coredata.dc:title: Tri-bore composite hollow fiber membranes consisting of nano CQDs and PolyActive™ for CO2 capture

coredata.dc:description: Due to the unique strengths of tri-bore hollow fiber membranes, we have developed PolyActive™/PDMS/PES tri-bore composite hollow fiber membranes comprising nano amino-functionalized carbon quantum dots (referred to as NCQDs thereafter) in this study for CO2 capture applications. The tri-bore polyethersulfone (PES) substrate was firstly optimized by investigating the effects of spinning conditions on membrane geometry and morphology with the aims to reduce outer-surface porosity and enhance mechanical strengths, followed by the incorporation of NCQDs to improve membrane hydrophilicity and minimize intrusion during the gutter-layer coating of polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS). Then, the coating conditions to deposit PolyActive™ as the selective layer on PDMS/PES hollow fibers were examined. The optimized PolyActive™/PDMS/PES (i.e., PA0.75/PDMS1/N1-PES) tri-bore hollow fiber membrane exhibits a CO2 permeance of 310 GPU. In pure gas tests, it achieves an ideal selectivity of 25.56 for CO2/N2, comparable to those traditional single-bore hollow fiber membranes in literature but with much stronger tensile properties for better handling. In mixed gas tests using a 15/85 CO2/N2 gas at 2 bar, the CO2 permeance and CO2/N2 selectivity only slightly decline to 262 GPU and 24.34, respectively, due to the sorption competition. In addition, the optimized membrane shows an almost stable long-term mixed-gas separation performance over 40 days. Its CO2 permeance slightly increases from 262 GPU to 303 GPU, while CO2/N2 selectivity minorly decreases to 22.1, confirming the membrane's potential for CO2 capture. To our best knowledge, this is the first attempt to design tri-bore hollow fiber membranes for CO2 capture.

head.ce:title: Tri-bore composite hollow fiber membranes consisting of nano CQDs and PolyActive™ for CO2 capture

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to the unique strengths of tri-bore hollow fiber membranes, we have developed PolyActive™/PDMS/PES tri-bore composite hollow fiber membranes comprising nano amino-functionalized carbon quantum dots (referred to as NCQDs thereafter) in this study for CO2 capture applications. The tri-bore polyethersulfone (PES) substrate was firstly optimized by investigating the effects of spinning conditions on membrane geometry and morphology with the aims to reduce outer-surface porosity and enhance mechanical strengths, followed by the incorporation of NCQDs to improve membrane hydrophilicity and minimize intrusion during the gutter-layer coating of polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS). Then, the coating conditions to deposit PolyActive™ as the selective layer on PDMS/PES hollow fibers were examined. The optimized PolyActive™/PDMS/PES (i.e., PA0.75/PDMS1/N1-PES) tri-bore hollow fiber membrane exhibits a CO2 permeance of 310 GPU. In pure gas tests, it achieves an ideal selectivity of 25.56 for CO2/N2, comparable to those traditional single-bore hollow fiber membranes in literature but with much stronger tensile properties for better handling. In mixed gas tests using a 15/85 CO2/N2 gas at 2 bar, the CO2 permeance and CO2/N2 selectivity only slightly decline to 262 GPU and 24.34, respectively, due to the sorption competition. In addition, the optimized membrane shows an almost stable long-term mixed-gas separation performance over 40 days. Its CO2 permeance slightly increases from 262 GPU to 303 GPU, while CO2/N2 selectivity minorly decreases to 22.1, confirming the membrane's potential for CO2 capture. To our best knowledge, this is the first attempt to design tri-bore hollow fiber membranes for CO2 capture.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: To address the issue of rapid climate changes due to the presence of rich CO2 in air, novel CO2 capture and storage technologies for industries such as cement and steel manufacturing, and power plants have received global attention [1–4]. Traditional CO2 capture methods like chemical absorption and cryogenic separation have the drawbacks of energy intensive, environmental unfriendly and large footprints [5–7]. In comparison, membrane technology offers advantages of high energy efficiency, small footprint, and ease of operation [7–9]. Because of the higher surface area to volume ratio per module and smaller footprint per system, hollow fiber membranes are more often used than flat sheet membranes for traditional gas separations such as O2/N2 and H2/N2 separations [10,11]. However, the conventional single-bore polymeric hollow fiber membranes may suffer from weak tensile properties under high-speed operations and large pressure drops in long modules [12–14]. In addition, the relationships between gas permeance and selectivity [15] and between separation performance and material costs [16–18] make it difficult to manufacture the entire hollow fiber membranes completely from a high separation performance but costly polymeric material.

To tackle this issue, tri-bore composite hollow fiber (HF) membranes with a triangular cross-section may offer a compelling solution. They may not only provide higher tensile properties and surface-to-volume ratios but also a larger packing density and more effective surface area compared to the single-bore ones [19–22]. These enhanced factors are essential to improve the sustainability and durability of membranes for CO2 capture applications. In addition, this configuration may prevent hollow fibers from sucking up epoxy during membrane module packing [23]. Comparing to other multi-bore hollow fiber membranes, triangle-shaped tri-bore hollow fibers have a more uniform wall thickness [24,25] and have been used for ultrafiltration, forward osmosis, pervaporation, and membrane distillation applications with superior mass transfer properties and tensile properties [19–24].

In this study, polyethersulfone (PES) was chosen as the substrate material because it has been widely used as substrates for various membrane applications due to its relatively low cost, good chemical and physical properties. PDMS and its derivatives were employed as the gutter layer because of their high permeability, good coatability and adhesion properties. The rubbery PDMS material has been broadly utilized to effectively seal pinholes on various substrates for gas separation membranes [7,10,16,25]. Generally, 2-layer PDMS/PES composite hollow fiber membranes can be manufactured before or after the module fabrication by many coating methods [10,14,16,25], while they are often produced by a dip-coating method prior to the fabrication of 3-layer (i.e., selective-layer/PDMS/PES) composite hollow fiber membranes to avoid fibers sticking together. Dip-coating is generally carried out by simply immersing the hollow fiber substrates into a pre-crosslinked PDMS coating solution, making the process easily scalable. However, precise control over the gutter layer coating remains challenging. The gutter layer plays a crucial role in supporting the ultra-thin selective layer to have a high gas permeance and selectivity. Poorly optimized gutter layers often result in structural defects and high gas transport resistance that compromise the overall membrane performance.

The intrusion of PDMS solutions into porous substrates is one of the challenges when fabricating the 2-layer composite membranes for gas separation. It can severely affect both permeance and selectivity depending on substrate materials and structures because of the enhanced gas transfer resistance [25–28]. Various approaches have been attempted to minimize the intrusion phenomena such as (1) annealing to reduce the pore sizes of substrates [29]; (2) optimization of casting or spinning parameters such as solvent chemistry, take-up speed and drying conditions to lower substructure resistance or downsize substrate pores [26,30]; (3) the use of highly viscous PDMS solutions to retard the PDMS penetration [31]; (4) the employment of a pre-wetting agent such as water [30] and FC-72 [32,33] to pre-fill the substrate holes. Although these methodologies work, annealing takes time and consumes energy, while FC-72 is a greenhouse gas harmful for global warming [34]. The adoption of water as a pre-wetting agent was found ineffective in our preliminary trials because of the complex structure and hydrophobic nature of tri-bore PES substrates.

The incorporation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) into the PDMS layer provides a potential solution to overcome this issue. CQDs possess high hydrophilicity and tunable surfaces, embedding them not only can alter the hydrophobic nature of PES substrates but also mitigate the intrusion of PDMS/hexane solutions, thereby reducing the gas transport resistance across PES substrates. In fact, CQDs have been embedded in many modern mixed matrix membranes (MMMs) because they are nano-sized and zero-dimensional nanoparticles with high compatibility, chemical inertness, tunable hydrophilicity, and surface functionality [35–37]. These unique characteristics would effectively render MMMs with less risks of particle agglomeration and interfacial defects, as observed in traditional MMMs [38].

The poly(ethylene oxide)-poly(butylene terephthalate) (PEO-PBT) block copolymer, commercially known as PolyActive™, was chosen as the selective layer material because it has an impressively high CO2 permeability of 115 Barrer and a CO2/N2 selectivity of 50 [39–41]. PolyActive™ is a PEO–PBT multiblock copolymer consisting of both thermoplastic and elastomeric behavior. The hydrophobic PBT segment provides mechanical strength while the flexible PEO segment offers mechanical flexibility and high affinity with CO2. A PolyActive™ grade of poly1.5k was used in this study because of its commercial availability and good solubility in common solvents [26].

Therefore, the objectives of this study are to reveal the science and engineering on design of PolyActive™/PDMS/PES tri-bore composite hollow fiber membranes by incorporating carbon quantum dots (CQDs) into the PES support layer for CO2 capture. The tri-bore PES substrate was firstly optimized by studying the effects of spinning parameters on membrane geometry and morphology to reduce outer-surface porosity and enhance mechanical strengths. Then, nano CQDs (referred to as NCQDs thereafter) were incorporated into the PDMS gutter layer to improve its hydrophilicity and reduce intrusion. Finally, a PolyActive™ selective layer was deposited on top of PDMS/PES hollow fibers and examined for various CO2 captures. To our best knowledge, this is the first attempt to design tri-bore hollow fiber membranes for CO2/N2 separation. This study may provide new insights to fabricate composite hollow fiber membranes for CO2 capture.

Polyethersulfone (PES, Ultrason E6020P) was kindly supplied by BASF. PolyActive™ (PA, 15000PEOT77PBT23) was purchased from PolyVation (Netherlands). N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP, ≥99.5 %) was sourced from MACRON, and triethylene glycol (TEG, 99 %) from Thermo Scientific. The Sylgard® 184 silicone elastomer kit was ordered from Dow Corning. Citric acid anhydrous (>99.5 %) was acquired from SHOWA Chemical Industry Co. Ethanolamine (EA, 99.5 %) and sodium hydroxide (98 %) were bought from Sigma-Aldrich. n-hexane was supplied by ECHO, chloroform were procured from Honywell (>99 %). Gases, including oxygen (O2, ≥99.5 %), nitrogen (N2, ≥99.995 %), carbon dioxide (CO2, ≥99.5 %), hydrogen (H2, ≥99.5 %), methane (CH4, ≥99.5 %) and a mixed gas (CO2/N2, 15/85 % and 50/50 % (v/v)) were provided by Linde LienHwa Industrial Gases Co., LTD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of amino-functionalized carbon quantum dots (NCQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The hydrophilic NCQDs were synthesized using a bottom-up method following our previous study [42]. Briefly, 5 g of citric acid and 1 g of ethanolamine were placed in an autoclave reactor and heated to 210 °C for 3 h, after which the reaction mixture was cooled down naturally to room temperature, yielding a brown paste. This paste was diluted with deionized water and adjusted to pH 9 using 5 M NaOH. The separation and purification process of NCQDs involved dialysis by means of a dialysis tubing cellulose membrane with a molecular weight cut-off of 1 kDa to remove unreacted precursors, followed by filtration through a microfilter of 0.22 μm to remove large impurities. The NCQD solution was then concentrated using a rotary evaporator and finally freeze dried. The average size of the NCQDs was about 4.5 nm in diameter, they showed a strong emission at a wavelength of 441 nm due to the quantum confinement effect [43]. Detailed characterizations can be found in Supplementary Fig. S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of tri-bore hollow fiber substrates
ce:section.ce:para: The tri-bore hollow fiber substrates were fabricated via a dry-jet wet spinning process as described elsewhere [22]. Fig. S2 illustrates the dimensions of the tri-bore spinneret. A pre-measured amount of NCQDs was dispersed in NMP using an ultrasonic processor (Q700, Qsonica) at 30 amplitudes for 3 h. Thereafter, PES and TEG were gradually added into the NCQDs/NMP mixture and vigorously stirred overnight. Subsequently, the dope solution and the bore fluid were loaded into ISCO pumps (500D, TELEDYNE ISCO) and degassed overnight at 65 °C prior to spinning. Detailed spinning parameters are depicted in Table 1 . A dope temperature of 65 °C was employed to ensure a stable and homogenous spinning solution, while a bore-fluid composition of NMP/H2O of 60:40 (wt./wt.) at 65 °C was used in order to induce delayed demixing and form a porous inner lumen surface with a low gas transport resistance [19–22]. In contrast, an outer coagulation temperature of 5 °C was utilized to induce rapid demixing and form a relatively dense outer surface to minimize the intrusion during the gutter-layer coating.

In tri-bore spinning, the air gap between the spinneret and the coagulation bath allows partial phase separation to occur prior to immersion, and the fiber elongation is controlled primarily by the dope flow rate and gravity-induced extension. The flow rate of bore fluid was crucial in determining the structure of tri-bore hollow fibers. Initially, the bore-fluid flow rates for the pristine PES hollow fibers were varied in the range of 3.25–4 mL/min. Subsequently, NCQDs were incorporated into the dope solution to further investigate their influence on hollow fiber structure and performance. Finally, the tri-bore hollow fiber substrates were spun at a fixed dope flow rate of 2 mL/min and an optimized bore-fluid flow rate of 4 mL/min. After spinning, the as-spun substrates were immersed in a water bath for three days, then cut into an appropriate length and underwent solvent exchange by immersion in methanol for 3 cycles (30 min/cycle), followed by immersion in hexane for another 3 cycles (30 min/cycle) [44]. Fresh methanol and hexane were used in each cycle to ensure the complete removal of residual solvents. The tri-bore substrates were then dried at room temperature and stored in a dry box for subsequent modifications and tests.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of PDMS/PES composite hollow fiber membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The PDMS solution was prepared by mixing the Sylgard® 184 silicone elastomer with the curing agent at a weight ratio of 10:1. The mixture was vigorously stirred for 3 min at room temperature, followed by thermal crosslinking at 70 °C for 8 min and 30 s. Subsequently, the viscous solution was diluted to 5 wt% using hexane, then a post-crosslinking process was conducted at room temperature for one month until the viscosity stabilized. The resulting 5 wt% PDMS solution was stored at −20 °C [31,45]. Before dip-coating the tri-bore hollow fiber substrates, the substrates were assembled into modules with the aid of metal rings and epoxy, and the pre-crosslinked 5 wt% PDMS solution was diluted to various desired concentrations using hexane. Each PDMS/PES composite membrane was fabricated by dip-coating a PES substrate in a specific PDMS solution for 3 s, the withdrawal distance was 5 cm, resulting in a controlled withdrawal speed of approximately 0.72 cm/s, followed by drying at room temperature for at least 24 h to ensure complete crosslinking.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of PA/PDMS/PES composite hollow fiber membranes
ce:section.ce:para: PolyActive™ solutions were prepared by dissolving PolyActive™ in a binary solvent system consisting of chloroform and n-hexane (50:50 vol%) at the desired concentrations (i.e., 0.25 %, 0.5 %, 0.75 %, and 1 % w/v). Each solution was heated to 40 °C and stirred continuously overnight to ensure complete dissolution. The PA/PDMS/PES composite membranes were fabricated by dip-coating the tri-bore PDMS/PES membranes in a specific PolyActive™ solution for 3 s followed by air-drying at room temperature for 24 h.

The pure gas permeation tests were performed using the system illustrated in Fig. S3. Each test module contained a single tri-bore hollow fiber membrane with an effective length of 5 cm. Pure gas tests were conducted by feeding the gas under an outside-in configuration at a feed pressure of 1 bar and 25 °C. The system was purged several times to remove residual gases. Before measuring gas permeance, the membrane was stabilized for 20 min to reach steady-state conditions. The gas permeance and selectivity were calculated using the following equations:

The apparatus for mixed gas tests is shown in Fig. S4. A CO2/N2 mixture of 15/85 v/v was used as the feed on the shell side of the membrane at 2 bar, while the permeate (i.e., the lumen side of the membrane) was open to atmosphere pressure. The flow rates of the permeate and retentate were monitored by a digital bubble flow meter. The flow rate of the retentate controlled by a diaphragm valve was adjusted to achieve a stage cut of <0.05 to minimize the effect of concentration polarization. Here, the stage cut was defined as the ratio of the permeate flow at the outlet to the feed flow rate [46]. Before measurements, the system was stabilized for 20 min to ensure that steady-state conditions were reached. The permeance was measured by the flow rate of the permeate, while the compositions of the permeate and retentate were analyzed by gas chromatography (GC) using the following equations:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the mechanisms for membrane formation
ce:section.ce:para: The viscosities of dope solutions were measured by a rheometer (RST-CPS, Brookfield) with a cone plate of 25 mm at a shear rate of 100 s−1 and 25 °C. The dynamic phase inversion of both PES and PES/NCQD systems was observed using a phase contrast optical microscope following our previous work [47]. In brief, a small drop of the dope solution (∼10 μL) was placed between two glass slides with a gap height of approximately 100 μm, ensuring a uniform sample thickness for observation. Subsequently, a droplet of DI water was carefully added at the edge of the glass slides to initiate the phase inversion. The water spontaneously infiltrated the gap between the slides due to the capillary force, contacting the polymer solution and triggering the phase inversion process. The nonsolvent intrusion process was recorded by a camera and analyzed.

Cross-sectional samples of membranes were prepared by fracturing them in liquid nitrogen to ensure clean and precise breaks. Surface samples of tri-bore hollow fibers were carefully cut from the fibers to preserve the structural integrity. The morphologies of tri-bore substrates and composite membranes were examined using a field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, JEOL 6500F). Elemental analyses of membranes were conducted using energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) to provide detailed material compositions and distributions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical properties and dynamic water contact angles
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanical properties of tri-bore hollow fibers, including Young's modulus, tensile strain at break, and maximum tensile stress, were evaluated using an Instron universal testing system (34SC-05, Instron). Each sample was prepared with a length of 10 cm. The initial gauge length and extension speed were set to 50 mm and 10 mm/min, respectively. A minimum of five measurements were conducted for each membrane to ensure statistical reliability.

The dynamic contact angles of water on tri-bore substrates were measured using a tensiometer (DCAT 25, DataPhysics Instruments) via the force tensiometry method at 25 °C [48]. A tri-bore hollow fiber was mounted on a fiber holder connected to the force sensor of the instrument. The fiber was automatically immersed into DI water and subsequently withdrawn. The advancing and receding contact angles of water on a membrane were automatically calculated by the instrument's software to estimate its surface properties under dynamic conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of bore-fluid flow rate on PES tri-bore hollow fibers
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 shows the effect of bore-fluid flow rate on the morphology of tri-bore PES hollow fiber membranes. At a low bore-fluid fluid flow rate, the fiber exhibits an approximately round geometry with three oval-shape bore channels. The fiber geometry gradually transitions to a triangular one comprising three round bore channels with an increase in bore-fluid fluid flow rate (Fig. 1a–d). Bore fluid flow rates exceeding 4 mL/min are too high to form a continuous wall and are therefore not discussed further. The changes in fiber geometry from round to triangular with increasing the bore-fluid flow rate can be explained as follows. The phase inversion process inside the lumen channels is accelerated at higher bore-fluid flow rates. This leads to the rapid formation of three round bore surfaces when the bore-fluid contacts with the dope solution. As the nascent fiber enters the air gap region, its viscoelastic properties cause rapid chain relaxation, die swell and undergo contour rearrangement [49–51]. Thus, the outer surface undergoes shrinkage due to an imbalance between solvent out-flux and nonsolvent in-flow [52,53]. It is then gradually stretched into a triangular shape around the rigid, rapidly formed bore channels [21,22]. Because the bore fluid contains 60 wt% NMP, the inner surfaces of all membranes are porous due to delayed demixing (Fig. 1e–f). Meanwhile, the number of pores on the outer surface slightly declines as the bore-fluid flow rate increases, presumably due to the shrinkage of the outer surface [54]. As discussed by Luo et al. [21], triangular tri-bore hollow fibers may outperform round ones in terms of maximum packing density, greater effective membrane area, and higher flux. Table 2 summarizes the geometric properties of tri-bore hollow fiber (HF) membrane substrates spun from different bore-fluid flow rates. The effective cross-sectional area refers to the area occupied by the fiber material itself in the cross-section, excluding the tri-bore area.

As shown in Table 2, when the bore fluid flow rate is 3.25 mL/min, the inner perimeter is 11.72 mm, increasing to 12.90 mm as the flow rate reaches 4 mL/min. As the bore fluid flow rate increases from 3.25 to 4 mL/min, the effective cross-sectional area occupied by the fiber material itself significantly decreases. This reduction primarily results from the geometric transition from a circular to a triangular shape. Due to its unique triangular geometry, the tri-bore hollow fiber membrane would not only provide a larger inner flow channel within the same fiber volume but also a lower pressure drop in long modules, thereby enhancing the overall mass transfer efficiency. Finally, a bore fluid flow rate of 4 mL/min, which produces triangular tri-bore hollow fibers with a dense outer surface, is adopted in the following studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of NCQD loading on morphology and phase inversion
ce:section.ce:para:
Figs. 2 and 3
illustrate the cross-sectional structures and surface morphologies of PES hollow fibers spun from different NCQD loadings. As shown in Fig. 2a–d, the outer membrane geometry transitions from triangular to circular as the NCQD loading increases from 0 to 3 wt%. In addition, both the number and length of macrovoids in the spoke region decrease. These micro- and macro-structure changes arise from a higher dope viscosity that results in a higher stress profile within the spinneret and a larger die swell after exiting from the spinneret [55–59]. As confirmed in Table 1, the addition of NCQDs increases the dope viscosity from 11,664 to 15,074 cP. Die swell occurs instantaneously when a highly viscous polymeric melt or fluid is forced through a die [50,55–59], the release of shear stresses accumulated within polymeric chains from oriented molecules to random coil ones becomes more dominant at a higher dope viscosity. Thus, it leads to form a circular cross-section when it contacts a cold external coagulant, as observed in Fig. 2d. In summary, the balance among die swell, surface tension, and surface shrinkage ultimately governs the fiber transition from triangular to circular geometries [22]. Meanwhile, a higher dope viscosity associated with a higher NCQD loading also hinders nonsolvent intrusion [52,53,60] and retards macrovoid formation [52,58–60], as illustrated in Fig. 2e–h and discussed later.

Fig. 3 shows the SEM images of cross-sectional and surface morphology of tri-bore PES hollow fiber substrates, where n is the NCQD concentration varying from 0 to 3 wt%. The following observations can be drawn. The inner edges of all membranes appear more porous than their outer edges. This is consistent with our previous hypothesis that a weak bore-fluid coagulant containing 60 wt% NMP at 65 °C would induce delayed demixing at the inner surface, while a water coagulation bath at 5 °C would result in rapid demxing and form a denser outer surface. The presence of NCQDs also contributes to a looser structure at the outer edges (Fig. 3e–h). These morphological changes, including improved surface porosity and a loosened inner structure, should supposedly significantly increase gas permeance. However, as the NCQD concentration increases, the solution viscosity also rises. It would retard the non-solvent intrusion and reduce the number and development of macrovoids, particularly in the spoke regions as shown in Fig. 2e–h. Interestingly, as the NCQD loading increases, both the inner and outer surfaces become more porous (Fig. 3i–p).

To examine the impact of NCQD loading on the phase inversion behavior of PES and N3-PES systems, dynamic phase inversion analyses were conducted. As shown in Fig. 4 , PES undergoes rapid precipitation when its dope solution is exposed to the non-solvent (i.e., water), forming a thin solidification front within 1 s, followed by a rapidly expanding precipitation front. This fast precipitation rate results in the formation of large finger-like macrovoids [58,60,61]. In contrast, the solidification front in the N3-PES system becomes noticeably thicker (i.e., denser and darker), and the overall precipitation rate slows down. This not only hinders solvent exchange but also effectively suppresses macrovoid formation [60,62].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical properties and dynamic contact angles
ce:section.ce:para: Table 3 compares the mechanical properties (1) between the single-bore and tri-bore PES hollow fiber substrates as well as (2) among tri-bore substrates as a function of NCQD loading. The tri-bore hollow fiber substrates generally have higher mechanical properties than the single-bore ones due to the formation of a spoke region. In addition, the tri-bore substrate incorporated with 1 wt % NCQDs has the best overall mechanical properties compared to others possibly because of (1) greater interactions among polymeric chains, solvents, and NCQDs, and (2) less inner (Fig. 3i–l) and outer surface porosity (Fig. 3m–p). Interestingly, the tri-bore substrate comprising 3 wt % NCQDs has the highest Young's modulus but the lowest tensile stress and strain. The highest Young's modulus may result from the reduced number of macrovoids in the spoke region (Fig. 2e–h), while the lowest tensile stress and strain may arise from its largest inner and outer surface porosity (Fig. 3i–p). Therefore, the tri-bore PES hollow fiber substrate loaded with 1 wt % NCQDs is the optimal substrate.

In terms of wettability, the advancing angle represents the repellency of a water droplet on the material surface in its initial state, which can truly reflect the material's wettability. The advancing contact angle decreases from 97.56° to 91.45° because of the addition of hydrophilic NCQDs into the PES substrates. The receding angle also decreases from 33.98° to 24.75° at a 1 wt% NCQD loading but increases to 43.04° at a 3 wt% NCQD loading. The former decrease is due to the enhanced surface hydrophilicity, while the latter increase may arise from the changes in surface energy and porosity [63].

Fig. 5 a shows the CO2 and N2 permeances of PES substrates spun from different bore-fluid flow rates. Consistent with our SEM observation, both CO2 and N2 permeances increase as the bore-fluid flow rate increases from 3.25 to 3.75 mL/min but slightly decreases when the flow rate further increases to 4 mL/min. The initial increase arises from a thinner wall (Fig. 1a–c) which reduces gas transfer resistance, while the subsequent decrease results from a reduction in outer surface porosity (Fig. 1l). Since Knudsen diffusion is dominant in these substrates because their O2/N2 selectivity varies from 0.92 to 0.93. These substrates must be coated for potential gas separation applications.

After dip-coating these substrates in a 1 wt% PDMS/hexane solution, Fig. 5b and c displays their CO2, O2 and N2 permeances and ideal CO2/N2 and O2/N2 selectivity as a function of bore-fluid flow rate. With an increase in bore-fluid flow rate from 3.25 to 4.0 mL/min, the CO2 permeance slightly increases from 1184 to 1270 GPU, while the ideal CO2/N2 selectivity climbs visibly from 6.05 to 8.13 (Fig. 5c). Therefore, a bore-fluid flow rate of 4 mL/min is chosen for the subsequent studies. Fig. 5d exhibits the CO2 permeance as a function of PDMS concentration coated on this PES tri-bore substrate. If the PDMS concentration is 0.5 wt%, the PDMS/PES tri-bore membrane has a poor CO2/N2 selectivity of 2.73 possibly because (1) it is too diluted to effectively coat the substrate and form a useful PDMS gutter layer and (2) the intrusion of PDMS into the substrate increases the substructure resistance and lowers the selectivity [16,25,30,64]. In contrast, if the PDMS concentration is 1.5 wt%, the CO2 permeance drops to 503 GPU because of forming an overly thick PDMS layer, Therefore, 1 wt% PDMS is employed for the following studies to produce useful gas separation membranes.

Fig. 6 a and b displays the CO2 permeance and CO2/N2 selectivity as a function of NCQD concentration using 1 wt% PDMS as the coating solution. The PES substrate loaded with 1 wt% NCQDs has the best separation performance across all samples. The CO2 permeance increases from 1270 to 2023 GPU, while the CO2/N2 selectivity rises from 8.13 to 10.23 compared to the pristine PES/PDMS membrane. These data reconfirm our hypothesis that solely relying on spinning conditions and PDMS concentration to overcome intrusion is insufficient. In addition to the surface pores on PES substrates, the interactions among PES, PDMS and hexane also play an important role in facilitating the intrusion because they are hydrophobic. The addition of a small amount of hydrophilic NCQDs into the dope solution is essential to retard the intrusion. However, a further increase in NCQD loading does not improve the performance because its higher surface porosity (Fig. 3p) results in a greater PDMS intrusion. Thus, not only does the CO2/N2 selectivity drop to 2.81 but also the CO2 permeance is lowered to 1632 GPU with a loading of 3 wt% NCQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: EDX scans of intrusion phenomenon in PDMS/N-PES membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 shows SEM-EDX line scans of silicon, carbon, sulfur, and oxygen near the outer edges of PDMS/PES hollow fiber substrates, confirming the PDMS intrusion into the substrates. A comparison of silicon profiles between hollow fibers with and without NCQD loading indicates that the degree of PDMS intrusion decreases when 1 wt% NCQDs are added into the substrates. The silicon peak is weaker across the substrate without NCQDs, showing that PDMS can easily penetrate this substrate because PES, PDMS and hexane are hydrophobic, leading to a uniform silicon distribution. In contrast, the hydrophilic NCQDs retard hexane and PDMS from entering the substrates, thus PDMS builds up mainly on the outer edge with minimal intrusion when 1 wt% NCQDs are added.

To identify the preferred conditions for coating the PolyActive™ layer on top of PDMS1/N1-PES membranes, Fig. 8 a and b compares the effects of its coating concentration and temperature on CO2 separation performance, respectively. When the coating was conducted at room temperature, Fig. 8a shows that both CO2 and N2 permeances decrease with increasing PolyActive™ concentration, while the CO2/N2 selectivity varies from 11 to 13.51. The permeance decline arises from a thicker PolyActive™ deposition, while the unimpressive selectivity may result from dewetting, a defect caused by the rupture of a liquid thin film during membrane formation [65]. Dewetting occurs due to the lack of physical crosslinks or entanglements within the polymer chains in solutions, preventing the formation of a stable liquid thin film and consequently reducing selectivity. By raising up the coating temperature to 40 °C, the CO2/N2 selectivity significantly improves. The CO2/N2 selectivity and CO2 permeance of the N1-PES membrane coated with 1 % PolyActive™ increase from 13.51 to 29.32 and from 79 to 223 GPU respectively compared to those coated at room temperature.

Therefore, the evaporation rate plays a crucial role in membrane formation. A high coating solution temperature enhances the evaporation rate, which accelerates the solidification process. In addition, the presence of PBT segments in PolyActive™ would boost adhesion between the PDMS gutter layer and the selective layer because both are hydrophobic. Furthermore, their intimate interactions may facilitate the migration of most PEO segments in PolyActive™ toward the outer surface, forming a CO2-philic layer. As a result of this design strategy, the newly developed PolyActive™/PDMS/PES composite hollow fiber membrane has a CO2/N2 selectivity of 25.56, and a CO2 permeance of 310 GPU after coating with 0.75 % PolyActive™ at 40 °C. For other gas pairs, such as CO2/CH4 and CO2/H2, Table 4 shows their separation performance. Generally, they are lower than the inherent selectivity reported in literature (i.e., 17 and 10.2, respectively [39–41]) due to the imperfect selective layer and substructure resistance [16,25,64]. However, the CO2/N2 selectivity is comparable to those asymmetric hollow fibers in literature and will be discussed in the benchmarking section later.

Compared to the pure gas results conducted at a trans-membrane pressure of 1 bar, Table 4 also shows the mixed gas results using 15/85 and 50/50 CO2/N2 feeds at a trans-membrane pressure of 2 bar. They have slightly lower CO2 permeances of 262 ± 21 and 266 ± 11 GPU, respectively, while the measured CO2/N2 selectivity appears lower than under pure gas conditions, statistical analyses by means of ANOVA (N > 5) confirm that the selectivity differences are not significant. The performance declines may be attributed to (1) sorption competition [7,46,66,67]; (2) membrane densification at a relatively higher pressure that tightens the chain packing and reduces the free volume for gas transport [7,68]; and (3) CO2-induced plasticization [7,46]. Briefly, a higher CO2 concentration would dominate the adsorption due to its high polarity and strong affinity with the polar groups of the PEO segment, leading to a relatively higher CO2 permeance. Similarly, the CO2 induced plasticization loosens the polymer chains for gas transport. Consequently, the mixed gas results using 50/50 CO2/N2 as the feed has a slightly higher CO2 permeance than those using a 15/85 CO2/N2 feed.

Additionally, the membrane was tested under humidified 15/85 CO2/N2 feed conditions (50 % RH), yielding a CO2 permeance of 413 GPU, a N2 permeance of 20 GPU, and a CO2/N2 selectivity of 20.24. The enhanced CO2 transport is attributed to water-induced swelling of the PEO segments, which increases chain mobility and free volume. These data illustrate the separation performance of a membrane depends on the balance among competitive adsorption, plasticization, and membrane densification.

Fig. 9 shows the long-term performance stability of the optimal membrane under a trans-membrane pressure of 2 bar at 25 °C. A 50/50 CO2/N2 ratio was used for the first 20 days, followed by a 15/85 ratio until the aging process reached 40 days. The CO2/N2 selectivity shows a slight decrease from 24.34 to 22.1, while the CO2 permeance exhibits a minor increase from 262 to 303 GPU after 40 days. Interestingly, these trends are quite different from those aging data observed under pure gas tests for dense membranes [69,70] and hollow fibers [71], suggesting the presence of CO2 in the feeds still induces minor plasticization and retards the aging phenomena [72]. Moreover, although the feed was not intentionally humidified during the long-term test, residual humidity in the gas or environment may contribute to moisture-induced swelling of the hydrophilic PolyActive™ layer, which could further increase CO2 transport but also compromise size-sieving ability, leading to the observed selectivity decline. More efforts will be devoted to understanding this phenomenon in the future. Nevertheless, the newly developed tri-bore membrane demonstrates a reasonably stable CO2/N2 selectivity and CO2 permeance over 40 days. This supports the presence of combined effects from plasticization and humidity in long-term or practical operating environments.

Fig. 10 plots the separation performance of PolyActive™/PDMS/PES tri-bore hollow fiber membranes as a function of PolyActive™ concentration in the coating solutions, and compares them with a modified Robeson's upper bound [73], where gas permeance is used instead of permeability. The newly developed tri-bore hollow fiber membrane follows the traditional trade-off relationship between selectivity and permeance, but the separation performance is below the 2008 upper bound and commercially available flat-sheet ones [1,7,73]. However, they have at least comparable performance with those single-bore hollow fiber membranes, as shown in Fig. 10 and listed in Table 5 . Because tri-bore hollow fibers have advantages of higher tensile properties and possibly better packing density than single-bore ones, they may offer a promising candidate to compete with those large-footprint flat-sheet membranes for CO2 capture. Future works will aim to improve the CO2 permeance and CO2/N2 selectivity with aid of nanoparticles, to thinner the PolyActive™ layer and to minimize the CO2 induced plasticization.

We have developed PolyActive™/PDMS/PES tri-bore composite hollow fiber membranes comprising NCQDs for CO2 capture because of their superior tensile properties. The tri-bore composite hollow fiber membranes consisted of a PES substrate incorporated with NCQDs, a PDMS gutter-layer, and a PolyActive™ selective layer. The optimized tri-bore membrane had a CO2 permeance of 310 GPU and an ideal CO2/N2 selectivity of 25.56 in pure gas tests.
(1)
The bore-fluid flow rate played an important role in determining membrane geometry, pore distribution, and gas permeability. An optimal bore flow rate of 4 mL/min facilitated the formation of a stable triangular geometry, increasing the effective inner surface area and enhancing gas transfer efficiency.
(2)
The incorporation of 1 wt% NCQDs effectively enhanced membrane hydrophilicity, reduced PDMS intrusion, and improved CO2/N2 selectivity and gas permeance.
(3)
The coating temperature of the PolyActive™ selective layer was critical for its structural integrity and overall membrane performance. A coating temperature of 40 °C accelerated solvent evaporation, leading to a more uniform coating layer.
(4)
Mixed gas tests under a higher trans-membrane pressure showed slightly lower CO2 permeance and CO2/N2 selectivity than pure gas results.
(5)
The optimized membrane also demonstrated stable performance under mixed gas tests (50/50 and 15/85 CO2/N2) and during long-term operation over 40 days, confirming its effectiveness.
To effectively compete with flat-sheet membranes for CO2 capture, future efforts on hollow fiber membranes should aim to improve CO2 permeance and CO2/N2 selectivity.
